shortDescription.text: IDOR via client-controlled user_id parameter overriding session identity

fullDescription.text: The GET /logs endpoint accepts a user_id query parameter that overrides the authenticated session user. Any authenticated user can read audit logs of any other user by supplying an arbitrary user_id value, violating the principle of least privilege and breaking tenant isolation.

shortDescription.text: SQL injection via string interpolation in get_user_logs()

fullDescription.text: The get_user_logs() function constructs a SQL query using Python f-string interpolation with an unsanitized user_id value. Because user_id originates from the request query string (an attacker-controlled source), this enables arbitrary SQL injection — including UNION-based data exfiltration, blind boolean/time-based extraction, and potential command execution depending on the database driver.

shortDescription.text: Verbose error message leaks internal details to the client

fullDescription.text: The exception handler returns the raw exception string to the HTTP response via f"Error loading logs: {e}". This can expose database table names, query structure, file paths, and stack trace fragments to an attacker, aiding further exploitation.

message.text: The user_id parameter from request.args overrides session['user']['id'], allowing any authenticated user to access another user's audit logs. Fix: always derive user_id from the server-side session and ignore client-supplied values, or enforce authorization checks comparing the requested user_id against the session identity.

get_user_logs() builds a SQL query via f-string interpolation with attacker-controlled user_id. This permits full SQL injection. Fix: use parameterized queries — e.g. cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM audit_logs WHERE user_id = %s", (user_id,)).

Raw exception text is returned in the HTTP response body. When combined with the SQL injection above, this can leak database schema, query fragments, and driver internals. Fix: log the full exception server-side and return a generic error message to the client.
